title: Lighting for PT Equipment and Light Meters
narrationScript: Fluorescent penetrant testing happens in the dark for a reason. The indication you are hunting is a tiny patch of dye that glows under ultraviolet light. That glow has to compete with every stray bit of white light in the booth. So lighting in this method is not a comfort item. It is a measured, documented process variable, and there are two numbers you will live by. The ultraviolet intensity at the part must be high enough, and the visible light around it must be low enough. Here are the typical procedure values. For Type one work, the UV-A intensity at the examination surface must be at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter. The ambient white light in the darkened booth must be no more than about two footcandles, which is roughly twenty lux. For Type two visible dye work there is one number instead. At least one hundred footcandles of white light at the surface, which is about one thousand lux. Your written procedure governs the exact values, but these are the numbers most procedures use. Now the hardware. The classic UV-A source is the mercury vapor lamp behind a deep violet filter. It produces strong ultraviolet centered near three hundred sixty five nanometers, but it needs time. A mercury arc must warm up and stabilize before its output is steady, commonly five to ten minutes, and if it loses power it has to cool down before it will strike again. Never measure and never inspect under a mercury lamp that has not stabilized. The newer source is the LED UV-A lamp. It reaches full output quickly and runs cooler, but it is not automatically acceptable. Aerospace and other specifications require LED lamps to be certified and checked like any other source, and your procedure may name the approved models. Both kinds carry a filter or lens that must be clean and intact. A cracked filter on a mercury lamp can leak hazardous short wave ultraviolet, so a cracked filter takes the lamp out of service immediately. You prove the numbers with two meters. A UV-A radiometer reads intensity in microwatts per square centimeter. A white light photometer reads footcandles or lux. Some instruments combine both. The rules for using them are the same. The sensor goes at the examination surface, facing the lamp, at the distance you will actually inspect. Intensity falls off fast with distance, roughly with the square, so a lamp that delivers well over the minimum up close can be far below it at arm's length. Many shops verify intensity at fifteen inches as a standard check distance. Meters are calibrated on a schedule, commonly every six months, traceable to a national standard, and the calibration record is part of your paperwork. When do you check? Typical procedures call for verifying ultraviolet intensity at the start of each shift or each day, after a bulb or lamp change, after a lamp is dropped or relocated, and any time output is in doubt. Ambient white light is measured with the booth set up exactly the way you inspect. Door position, curtains, monitor screens, and indicator lights all count toward the reading. Your own eyes are part of the system too. Coming from shop light into a darkened booth, give your eyes time to adapt, commonly at least a minute and often several, before you evaluate anything, and adapt again after every trip out. Do not wear photochromic lenses, the kind that darken automatically in sunlight, because the ultraviolet in the booth darkens them and robs you of the very light you need. Ordinary clear UV absorbing safety glasses are the right choice. They protect your eyes, and they cut the faint haze you see when ultraviolet makes the fluid inside your own eye fluoresce. And never stare into the beam of any ultraviolet source. One last habit separates professionals from everyone else. Treat every lighting number as data. Measure at the surface. Write the reading down. If it is below minimum, you stop. Clean the filter, check the bulb, seal the booth, and measure again. You never inspect your way past a failed light check, because every indication you would have found stays invisible in the wrong light.
imagePrompt: In a darkened industrial inspection booth bathed in violet UV light, a technician holds a digital radiometer sensor flat against a turbine blade surface to verify ultraviolet intensity at the working distance.

sections.heading: Two Numbers Rule the Booth
sections.body:
A fluorescent indication is a few micrograms of dye glowing under ultraviolet light. Whether you see it depends on a contest between that glow and every stray lumen of white light in the booth — so fluorescent PT lives and dies by two measured numbers.

First: **UV-A intensity at the examination surface of at least 1,000 µW/cm²** under typical procedures. Not at the lamp face, not somewhere in the booth — at the surface you are evaluating, at the distance you actually hold the lamp. Second: **ambient visible light of about 2 fc (roughly 20 lux) or less** in the darkened area. High UV through a leaky curtain fails the check; a pitch-black booth under a tired bulb fails it too — you need both numbers at once.

Visible-dye (Type II) work inverts the problem: red-on-white contrast needs ordinary light, and plenty of it — **at least 100 fc (about 1,000 lux) of white light at the surface**, the same target used for fine visual inspection. These are typical procedure values; your written procedure governs the exact limits and how often you must prove them.

sections.caption: The two measured conditions of a fluorescent booth — UV-A intensity at the surface and ambient white light — with the radiometer sensor at the working distance.

sections.heading: UV-A Sources: Mercury Vapor and LED
sections.body:
The legacy source is the **mercury-vapor lamp** behind a deep-violet filter that passes UV-A centered near **365 nm** and blocks most visible light. Mercury arcs need **warm-up** — commonly five to ten minutes — before output stabilizes, and a hot lamp that loses power must cool before it will restrike. Never measure intensity, and never inspect, under a mercury lamp that has not stabilized.

The **filter is a safety device**, not just an optical one. A cracked or missing filter can leak hazardous shorter-wavelength ultraviolet — a cracked filter takes the lamp out of service on the spot. Dirt, overspray, and penetrant film on the filter or reflector quietly eat output, which is why a low reading sends you to clean the lamp before you condemn the bulb.

**LED UV-A lamps** dominate new equipment: near-instant stabilization, cooler running, long life. They are not automatically acceptable — aerospace and similar specifications require LED sources to be certified for wavelength, beam profile, and stability, and your procedure may name the qualified models. Flood units light a whole booth or wash station, spot lamps and battery hand lamps serve field work — and every one of them answers to the same radiometer.

sections.heading: The Meters and How to Use Them
sections.body:
Two instruments prove the numbers. A **UV-A radiometer** reads irradiance in µW/cm². A **white-light photometer** reads illuminance in footcandles or lux. Combined units carry both sensors.

Using them well comes down to three habits. **Position:** the sensor goes at the examination surface, face toward the lamp, at the real working distance — a reading taken at the lamp face or halfway in between proves nothing. **Condition:** meters carry a calibration sticker, typically on a **six-month cycle** traceable to a national standard; an out-of-date meter invalidates every reading it takes. **Timing:** verify UV intensity at the start of each shift or day under typical procedures, after a bulb or lamp change, after a lamp is dropped or relocated, and any time output is suspect. Measure ambient light with the booth exactly as you inspect — curtains drawn, door set, monitor screens and indicator lights in their working state.

Record every reading. A lighting check that is not written down did not happen, and the recorded value is part of what makes your accept and reject decisions defensible.

sections.caption: The same lamp that passes at 15 inches fails farther out — intensity falls roughly with the square of distance, so verify at the distance you actually inspect.
Typical lighting acceptance values for PT (your written procedure governs).
rows: UV-A radiometer at the surface, working distance
Ambient white light in booth (Type I)
Photometer, booth in its real inspection state
Procedure requirement — give your eyes time
Traceable calibration record on the meter

sections.title: Protect the eyes that do the inspecting
sections.body: Never look into the beam of any UV source. A **cracked mercury-lamp filter** can leak hazardous short-wave UV — out of service immediately. Wear **clear UV-absorbing safety glasses**: they protect the cornea and cut the hazy glow caused by fluorescence inside your own eye. **Photochromic and tinted lenses are out** — booth UV darkens them and steals the very light you need to see indications.

sections.heading: Dark Adaptation and Booth Discipline
sections.body:
Walk from shop light into a darkened booth and your eyes are nearly useless for the first moments. Give them **dark adaptation time — commonly at least one minute, often several per procedure** — before evaluating, and re-adapt after every trip out. Fluorescent evaluation works at the dim edge of vision: anything that costs you sensitivity costs you flaws.

Housekeeping protects vision too. Penetrant smears on gloves, bench, and booth walls fluoresce and distract, so keep the booth clean and its surfaces dark and non-reflective. Dim or shroud monitor screens and indicator lights — they count toward the ambient reading. And remember that the daily system performance check, run on a known-defect comparator panel under the booth's own lights, is more than a chemistry check: it is the daily proof that your lamps, your meters, and your adapted eyes can still find the flaws you are paid to find.

interactive.title: Booth Check Before First Shift
interactive.intro: You open the fluorescent inspection booth for first shift. The procedure requires at least 1,000 µW/cm² UV-A at the examination surface, ambient white light of 2 fc or less, and meters in current calibration. Walk the verification the way a Level II should.

steps.prompt: You switch on the mercury-vapor UV lamp and the radiometer is in your hand. When do you take the intensity reading?
options: Immediately — arc output is highest at startup
After the lamp warms up and stabilizes, commonly five to ten minutes
After the first part is processed
Whenever the booth feels dark enough
steps.feedback: A mercury arc needs several minutes to stabilize; a reading taken cold means nothing. LED lamps stabilize much faster, but the warm-up your written procedure sets still applies.

steps.prompt: Where does the radiometer sensor go for the intensity reading?
options: Pressed against the lamp filter
Anywhere inside the booth
At the examination surface, facing the lamp, at the working distance
At the booth doorway, facing out
steps.feedback: The number that matters is irradiance where the part will be evaluated. Intensity falls roughly with the square of distance, so only a reading at the real working distance proves compliance.

steps.prompt: The meter reads 850 µW/cm² at your working distance. What now?
options: Inspect anyway — it is close to the minimum
Slide the sensor closer to the lamp until it reads 1,000
Stop. Clean the filter and reflector, check bulb age, and re-measure
Turn off the booth night-light and accept the reading
steps.feedback: A dirty filter or reflector is the most common cause of low output, so clean and re-measure before condemning the bulb. Chasing the number by measuring closer than the working distance just falsifies the check.

steps.prompt: UV now passes. The white-light photometer reads 5 fc ambient — and you notice the curtain is half open. What do you do?
options: Proceed; the UV reading is what matters
Close the curtain, seal the light leaks, and re-measure until ambient is 2 fc or less
Average the 5 fc with the darkest corner of the booth
Raise the UV intensity to compensate
steps.feedback: Both numbers must pass at once. Ambient is measured with the booth in its real inspection configuration, and white light washing out faint indications cannot be offset by more UV.

steps.prompt: Everything passes. The inspector walks in from the sunlit yard wearing photochromic glasses, ready to start. Your call?
options: Start immediately — the booth checks are done
Have them swap to clear UV-absorbing lenses and allow several minutes of dark adaptation first
Hand them the radiometer to hold during evaluation
Brighten the booth so the lenses stay clear
steps.feedback: Photochromic lenses darken under booth UV and cut visual sensitivity, and eyes coming in from daylight need dark adaptation time. The lighting system includes the inspector.

checkQuestions.question: What is the typical minimum UV-A intensity for Type I evaluation, and where is it measured?
options: At least 1,000 µW/cm², measured with a radiometer at the examination surface
At least 1,000 µW/cm², measured against the lamp filter
At least 100 µW/cm², measured anywhere in the booth
At least 2,000 µW/cm², measured at the booth doorway
checkQuestions.explanation: Typical procedures require 1,000 µW/cm² minimum at the surface being evaluated, proven with a calibrated UV-A radiometer at the actual working distance.

checkQuestions.question: During a lamp check you find a hairline crack in the mercury-vapor lamp's filter, though intensity still passes. What is the correct action?
options: Keep using it as long as the intensity reading passes
Tape over the crack and continue the shift
Remove the lamp from service — the filter blocks hazardous shorter-wavelength UV
Reserve the lamp for Type II inspections only
checkQuestions.explanation: The deep-violet filter keeps hazardous short-wave ultraviolet out of the booth; a cracked filter is a safety failure regardless of the UV-A intensity reading.

checkQuestions.question: A hand lamp reads 2,400 µW/cm² with the sensor 6 inches from the filter. Why can you NOT credit that reading for inspections done at arm's length?
options: Radiometers are only accurate at 6 inches
Intensity falls roughly with the square of distance, so it must be verified at the actual working distance
UV-A gains intensity with distance from the lamp
Readings are only valid when taken at the lamp face
checkQuestions.explanation: Irradiance drops steeply — roughly with the square of distance — so a lamp that passes at 6 inches can be far below minimum at the real working distance. Verify where you inspect.

checkQuestions.question: How is the ambient white-light limit (about 2 fc) in a fluorescent booth correctly verified?
options: Estimate it by eye after dark adaptation
Measure with the booth lights on for safety
Measure UV-A at the lamp face with the white lights off
Measure with a photometer at the examination surface with the booth in its real inspection configuration
checkQuestions.explanation: Ambient light is measured where the part is evaluated, with curtains, door, screens, and indicator lights exactly as they will be during inspection — every leak counts toward the reading.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about light meters used for PT is correct?
options: They are calibrated once at purchase and never again
They are calibrated on a schedule, commonly every six months, traceable to a national standard
Calibration is only needed when readings look wrong
Radiometers do not drift, so only photometers need calibration
checkQuestions.explanation: Typical practice puts radiometers and photometers on a traceable calibration cycle of about six months; an out-of-calibration meter invalidates the lighting checks it performed.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §4.2 (lighting for PT equipment and light meters)
Practices such as ASTM E1417 and ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 (typical lighting values)
Industry specifications for certified UV-A LED examination lamps (generic reference)
